Annotation: In a prouince of the Medes, wherof Rages was the head citie: for whẽ they came where Raguel dwelt, Tobias stayed there, and the Angel went to the citie of Rages where Gabelus dwelt. c. 9.
As one may say: such a one dwelleth in Rome, that dwelleth in anie part of Roman ia: in York, Lincolne, or Munmoth, that dwelleth in one of those shires.
Asmodeus, signifying Destroyer is a captaine or King of those diuels which specially destroy foules by the sinnes of the flesh, & afterward tormenteth both foules and bodies for the same sinnes.
As Moyses to the people Deut.13. and David to Salomon 3. Reg.2. so Tobias gave holie admonitions to his sonne: in al fourteen, noted in the inner margin.
The same doctrine of good workes, and reward, is taught David. v.24.
It perteyneth to good men among other things, to give notice and to dispose of their temporal goods by their last wil.
The Angel Raphael appearing in forme of a man prefigured our Saviour, who indeed became a verie man. S. Beda.
Raphael signifying medicine of God (S Gre. ho.34.) calleth himselfe Azarias, whose shape & visage he tooke upon him, which name also signifieth the helpe of God.
Angel Guardian.
S. Paul also calleth flesh of flesh therof, and Plinie lib. 9. c. 19.

Diuels who exalted themselves as equal with God, are iustly made subiect to corporal creatures. God concurring with natural causes, whose good pleasure is to instruments naturally ript, as when Christ gave sight to the blinde by putting clay on his eyes. Joan. 9. Sometimes more apt, as when he fed manie with few loaues, Joan 6. So the Angel by Gods appointment vsed this meanes to expel the diuel.
Into the place where good soules rested, none then hauing accesse into heauen. See Annot. Gen. 37.
The second night he asked and obtayned this grace; for he knew not his wife vntil the fourth night. v. 22.

A iustman afrayd, knowing what had chanced to those seuen husbands, which went in vnto her: and he began to feare left perhaps it might chance to him also in like manner: and when he doubted, and gaue no answer to him demanding, the Angel sayd to him: Feare not to giue her to this man, for to him fearing God is thy daughter due to be his wife: therfore an other could not haue her.
See c.6, v.8. Mystically it signified Christs passion whereby the diuel was expelled out of mens harts.
In the one familie there were no more children but one sonne, in the other one only daughter.
The Angel went to the citie it selfe called Rages, Tobias remaining in the territorie or prouince therof with Raguel, which place is also called Rages. c. 3. v.7.

Such of the Iewes as belieue in Christ hartily lament that he tarieth so long from their nation: Some more assuredly with old Tobias, others more doubtfully with his wife, expect his returne. S. Beda in Tobiam. Euen so the remnant of Catholikes in countries fallen to heresie haue greate sadnes and continuall sorow in their harts (Rom. 9.) wishing (with what temporall losse soeuer) the saluation of their brethren, kinsmen, and countriemen: some hoping more confidently & comforting others that Christ wil againe illuminate our whole nation, as light was restored to old Tobias.

It nothing disgraceth the sacred historie that a smal matter being also true is recorded with the rest, As no one letter nor any tisle of the law may be omitted. Mat. 5. Beda also expoundeth it mystically of Gods Preachers. S. Ierome dath the like. Isai 56, and S. Augustin also. 12. c. 16.

I offered thy prayer to our Lord. Here the Angel Raphael reporteth certaine good offices, which he had done for Tobias. He did other like for his sonne, and for Raguel, and his daughter, which are likewise recorded in this Book. And the whole world, especially Gods seruants, receiue continual great benefits by holie Angels. For first the office of Angels is, to assist, or be alwayes readie to, as most diligent seruitours of God, expecting what his diuine goodnes wil appoint them. Secondly, Angels offer the prayers of the faithful to God: so Raphael testifieth here himself, that he offered Tobias prayers to our Lord. Thirdly, Angels ayde and assist those that loue puritie of life, sincere seruice of God, hate vice, embrace vertue, and doe works of mercie. Fourthly, Angels exhort to good works, as in this chapter v. 6. 8. 9. 10. 18. Fiftly, they suggest and instruct what to doe. Sixthly, they expel diuels from persons and places. Seuently, they deliuer men from dangers and euils. Eighthly, when it redoundeth to the honour and more seruice of God, and good of the soule, Angels procure riches and wordly commodities. Ninthly, Good Angels also proue men for their mercement. Tenthly, after proofe of patience, fortitude, and other vertues, holie Angels comfort good men. Raphael encouraged old Tobias, shewed him how grateful his prayers with teares and works of mercie were in Gods sight.
Temporal affliction for the spiritual good of others.
His bodie being in captiuitie, yet his spirit was free to praise and thank God. S. Cyprian lib. de mortalitate.
He prophecieth the reedifying of the temple and citie of Ierusalem.
Onlie celestial Ierusalem shal be free from al tribulations.
No Christian is ignorant (sayth S. Augustin Epist. 6) that Alleluia is a voice of praise. In English it is: Praise ye the Lord with joy.
This can not be understood of the citie of Jerusalem but of the Church of Christ.
Gods servants must not only expect children that they doe iustice and almes-deeds, that they be mindful of what shal be commanded, but also seeke to know what they ought to doe.

A notable commendation of Tobias family & posteritie. And a plaine testimonie that true Religion never failed wholy in the tenne tribes, much lesse in the Kingdome of Iuda, least of al in the Church of Christ and new Testament.
An expresse figure of Antichrist 2.Thes.2. for whom al heretikes make way as precursors, for the singular man of sinne wil confesse no God but himselfe.

The high Priest managed also the temporal fayres of the common welth, at this time, by consent & commission of King Manasses, as is most probable these things happening shortly after his re-entrance. 2. Paral. 33.
The second part. Gods people are in great feare and distresse.
Holofernes could not be altogether ignorant of so famous a people as the Iewes, but in his rage marveling at their attempt demanding more particularly of mouth to resist him.
He seemeth to speake of the captiuitie of King Manasses, 2. Paral. 33. See the argument of this Book.
As Holofernes v. 5. demanded that which he in great part knew, so his men knowing Achior wel enough, in age demand, who he is that dare say such things?
Nabuchodonosors vassals would have no other God but him. And God Almightie is also ielous and wil have no God but himselfe.

God regardeth the pride of sinners, the humiliation of penitentes, the sanctitie of his Church in general, as well holie persons, as holie things, and true confidence in him, without presuming of mens power.
Gods seruants first of al before they fight, workes of pennace, came of their cause to God, then take weapons in hand, & expect opportunitie to encounter with the enimies.
A common practise of people in distresse to impute error to their Superiours.
In three special obseruances this holie widow led a most religious life: In much priuate prayers.
In wearing haire-cloth and in much fasting: as it were a perpetual lent, al daies but Sabbaths & feasts excepted.

S. Paul 1.Cor.10.v.10. alleadging that happened to the people of Israel in the desert, addeth this word destroyer, which is only in this place, and not Num. 11. nor 14. nor els in the old Testament: which is an other argument that this booke is Canonical.
Some thinke Abra was a proper name, but whether it was or no, it signifieth an handmaide of more honour, as we speake: A maide of honour.
Of such priuate oratories, as this our Sauiour seemeth to speake. Mat. 6. saying: Enter into thy chamber &c.
The zele of Simeon and Leui was commendable, but for diuers euil circumstances the fact was reproued by Iacob. Gen. 34 & 49.
She prayed, that Holofernes should be moued with her beautie & sweete speach: which he might be and not sinne, but he by his free wil sinning, God turned to the good of other.

God by this testified her holie intention in adorning herselfe. Not only certaine meates were forbidden by the Law, but in manner al the meates of the Gentils were vnlawful being offered to idols, for which cause Daniel and the three children would not eate of the Kings meates in Babylon.

To whom also our Lord gave beautie: because this trimming did not depend of sensualitie, but of vertue: and therfore our Lord amplified this beautie on her, that she might appeare to al mens eies of incomparable comlines.
S. Fulgentius probably iudgeth that Iudith was now about fourtie yeares old. Epist. 2. cap. 6.

Al that Iudith sayth is true in her sense as she meant it, but not in the vnderstanding of Holofernes and his souldiers, yet was no lesse lawful then that Iosue did in deceiuing the citizens of Hay. Iosue 8. S. Aug. q. 10. in Iosue.
See chap. 10. v. 5.
This puritie consisted in abstaining from meates forbid by the Law, or offered to Idols.
The fasting of one woman ouerthrew an innumerable host of drunkards. S. Amb. li. de ieiunio cap. Elia. c. 9.
If Bethulia had been taken, Ierusalem also had been in extreme danger: for which cause God had made more special promise to Dauid and others. Isaia 37. 38. &c.

Achior an Ammonite being truly converted and beleeuing in God, was admitted into the Church, notwithstanding that Ammonites and Moabites were excluded by the law Deut. 23, 3. which is therfore ment only of those persisting in their error.
Judith was a special figure of the B. Virgin Marie, to whom these praises pertayne in more eminent force, then to anie other creature.
S. Ephrem Ser. dez. ad Adventu, citeth this place as holy scripture, so testifying this Book to be canonical.
Such giants as were before Noe his flood, Gen. 5. Nor such as were after, Num. 13, Deut. 3.
Everlasting torments of fire & wormes perteyne to the damned bodies. S. Aug. li. 21. c. 4 &c. and greater paines to the damned soules, especially the losse of Gods vision.
Living in all 105 yeares, she widow about sixtie nine: for when she flew Holofernes she was about fourtie yeares old. c. 10, v. 18. & her husband was then dead three yeares & a halfe before.
The first & best remedie in distresse is to doe workes of penance for sinnes committed. 1. Cor. 11. v. 31.
Great confidence in Gods help when mans help fayleth: & indeed this meanes by mouing the Kings hart to grant Esthers petition was Gods special worke.
This prudence in delaying to propose her petition, increased the Kings desire to know it, moued him to promise more assuredly, & so bound him the more to accomplish it.

Gods eye which neuer sleepeth, saw what Aman intended: and for execution of his owne diuine iustice, vsed this meanes, by subtracting sleepe that time from the King, & inspiring his mind to heare the histories read, and to reward good seruice.
Ambitious men are most blind in conceite of their owne deserts, and fortunes.
Either they had read Gods promise to the Iewes Gen. 13. 15. &c. or conjectured this by humane prudence.
After three dayes fast of al the Iewes with prayers and other works of penance, Esther asked & obtained the fasting of the whole people, more precious to her then halfe of the Kingdom.
Horrour of a guiltie conscience is commonly the first torment of a sinner.
What little trust in false freindship, when bad men once fall into disgrace.
Such honour is due to Kings, yea though they be infidels, because they have terrestrial auctoritie from God.
S. Ierome here adverteth the reader that he found al hitherto in the Hebrew.

Mardocheus had this dreame before Amans advancement, of his owne and the peoples danger. Mardocheus was a good dragon, and Aman a bad one. Esther by her fountaine of teares and humble supplication, quenched a great flame of Gentils against the Iewes.
Here it appeareth that Aman was a favourer of traitours or perhaps of the fame conspiracie.
Great hurt to a King, that is ruled much by one counseller. Salomon teacheth, that where are manie counsellers, cogitations are confirmed. Prov.15. v. 22.
Idols are nothing in the world. 1. Cor.8. but bare imaginations of men. The Church vseth this prayer in the offertorie the 22. sunday after Pentecost; as a parcel of holy Scripture.
Not Amos the third of the lesser prophets, for this name is written in other letters in Hebrew: but one of the royal bloud, as S. Ierom testifieth.
The first part. The Kingdom of Iuda shal be captiue in Babylon, for their ingratitude towards Gods, and other sinnes.
Prince, Priest, and people are al sicke of ingratitude against God, & other iniquities.
Ierusalem defaced and destroyed.
God continually preferueth some holie seede, that his Church neuer faileth.

Much wickednes reigned in Ierusalem before their captiuitie in Babylon; but much more at Christs passion, when they persecuted him euen to the Crosse: and his disciples, and al Christians, til their citie was taken by the Romans, and the whole nation dispersed.

The Rabbins vnderstand those Iudges and Priests that gouerned the people after their deliuerie from captiuitie: but S. Ierom expoundeth this and the like places of the Apostles, and their successours.

It is not only certaine, but also euident that the prophet speaketh here, and in innumerable other places, of the Church of Christ, which is upon a mountaine, Matt. 5, vnto which al nations are gathered, and al the time of the new Testament is called the last, Io. 1. Ian. 2. because no time shal folow after this, but al eternitie.
The Iewes were reiected after Christs death: before which they were stil conserued: though often seuerely punished. And so now the Church of Christ shal neuer be reiected.
It is most absurde, and contrarie to this and other Scriptures, that Protestants feyne of great idolatrie in the Church.

This was fulfilled first in the captivity of Babylon, and more notoriously after Christs passion, in the destruction of Jerusalem, and dispersion of the Iewes, euen to this day: and yet forward, til nere the end of this world.

After the reduction of heathnish or heretical people to catholike religion, there wil be great want of spiritual pastours. Not al the Iewes that escaped temporal death in the destruction of Ierusalem, but those only shal be eternally saued, that belieuing shal be baptized and liue wel.
Ifaie of the tribe of Iuda here prophecieth the doleful song which Christ vttered weeping ouer Ierusalem, foreseeing & foretelling their destruction.

Al this sheweth that God only subtracting his protection, no man nor people is able to stand; of whose ruine God is not the authour, but only permitteth that they fal into sinnes, and so into their miseries.
An admonition to celebrate festiual dayes with holy religious exercises, and not to folow drunkennes nor other wicked or vayne things.
Grievous sinnes must be grievously punished. Such as was the sinne of the Iewes persecuting Christ.
Neither Ifaie, nor Moyses, nor anie other mortal man did euer see God in himselfe, but only shadowed in some manner.
Ifaie was not only an Evangelicall but also an Apostolicall Prophet, with whom God here treateth & proceedeth, as with an Apostle, saying: Whom shal I send.

Before this the Kings of Syria and of Israel had taken King Achaz in battell, and caried away great spoyles, 2. Paral. 28. Mystically this signified that heretikes of diuers sectes conspire together, to impugne the Catholike Church. Which they doe much afflict, and terrifie, but can neuer ouerthrow. S. Ierom.

Though Achaz was very wicked and committed idolatrie, yet he belieued in God Almightie, knowing that he ought not to tempt him. Vpon occasion of Gods mercie prouised without mans desert, God inspired the Prophet also to forshew the greater mysterie of Christs Incarnation, his conception & birth of a Virgin, for the redemption of al mankind.
The mystery here prophesied is of so great importance, as would require a very great book for full explication thereof.
Whether they seek to God in their extreme distress, not sincerely, but coasted, or seek worldly help, they shall not escape misery.

S. Matthew expoundeth this prophecy of Christ, first preaching in Galilee. Where his disciples believed in him & followed him. But after his passion few Jews believed in him, in comparison of the Gentiles.
He that is great, yea omnipotent God, is borne a little one in this world, and without violence conquereth & ruleth all the world.
God punishing sinners, and they not repenting, his just fury still increaseth: punishing eternally all those that never repent.

Whereas good laws are the stability of the commonwealth, wicked are the ruin thereof. Such as Jeroboam made forbidding to go to Jerusalem, and setting up golden calves in Dan, causing the people to serve them as the Gods of Israel. Such also as the Scribes and Pharisees made, wicked traditions, contraries to Gods commandments. Mat. 5.

The Jews are called a deceitful nation, because they broke their promise made to God, that they would serve him and keep his commandments. Exo. 5. Sennacherib not by his own power, but as Gods instrument afflicted the Israelites.
The Blessed Virgin. Christ our Saviour replenished with the seven guifts of the Holie Ghost, of whose infinite plenitude his servants participate as it pleaseth his divine spirit to impart.

Christ after his death, which to the world was ignominious, would be gloriously buried by very honourable persons, Joseph and Nicodemus, with abundance of most precious spices. Christs sepulchre still also remaineth glorious, honoured even by the Turkes: much more by Catholike Christians.
The Jews gave thanks for their delivery from captivity of Babylon; & much more the Church of Christ rendereth thanks for her deliverance from all sinnes.

Nemrod began the kingdom of Babylon (Gen. 10.) his sonne Belus did much augment it: & his sonne Nimus brought it to be a very great Empire & Monarchie. But at last, after 1240. years, it was overcome by Cyrus King of Persia.
Medes and Persians were called sanctified, in that they were the ministers of Gods justice in the ruine of Babylon.
After the slaughter there shal be so few Babylonians, or Chaldeans left alive, that one man shal be more precious then much fine gold.
An other citie was built of the fame name, but much lesse, and in an other place of Chaldea.

Isaie prophesied the destruction of Babylon above 100. yeares before the Iewes were caried thither captive, and their captivity indured 70. yeares. Which was released by Cyrus, after he had overcome the Babylonians. Yet this space of nere 200. yeares, is counted a short time in respect of so great a Monarchie as this was, which had continued above a thousand yeares, from the time of Ninus.
As Lucifer the greatest diuel, so Nabuchodonosor King of Babylon fel through pride into extreme miserie.
The miraculous destruction of the Assirians armie beseiging Ierusalem is recorded 4. Reg. 19.
The second commination is against the Philistians. Though Achaz was dead whom the Philistims feared, yet Ezechias a better King did afflict them more then the other had done.

The third commination was against the Moabites. Destruction made in the night, prevailed that they feared not the imminent danger, but to much the more they were afflicted, being sodainly oppressed with extreme miserie.
Miserie, even of enemies, moueth the charitable hart to compassion: so the Prophet lamenteth the Moabites affliction.

In the great miserie of the Moabites, the Prophet saw one special cause of consolation, that Christ the lambe of God, should be borne of their lineage. Of Ruth a Moabite, who was maried to Booz, and so was Dauids great grandmother. Ruth 4.
In three yeares, as the yeares of an hired servant, the glorie of Moab shal be taken away.
After that the Assirians had afflicted the Israelites, and their confederates, them selves were also afflicted.

The fift was against the Aethiopians and Aegyptians. The Aegyptians bid their messengers goe swiftly & tel the Iewes that they shal have present helpe according as they require. But the Prophet sheweth that the Aegyptians themselves would be ouerthrowne by the Assirians.
This final helpe of great importance, is that aide wherof Daniel prophesied ch.11. v.34.

Mathathias not of priuate spirite, but being general capitaine of the people, did this iustice according to the law, where it is commanded to kil the authours of false pretended religion. Deut.13. S. Cyprian Exhort. ad Martyr.

These are commended by S. Ambrose li 1. Offi. c. 40 and other Fathers, dying in the simplicitie of serpents. Yet ordinarie meanes of lawful warres are also vsed, by which God conserveth his Church from vtter ruine.
Assideans, otherwise called Esseni, not hypocritically as the Pharisees, nor erroneously as the Sadducees, but sincerely professed a peculiar holie rule of life.

In al affayres order is of singular importance, that euerie office be designed to fittest persons. Mathathias appointed Simon the chiefe for determining matters in counsel, & Iudas the first for execution.
He did not arrogate to himselfe to be chief, but being designed by his father, was so accepted by his brethren, and the good people ioyned with them in defence of the law of God.
Iudas had foure battels, and victories, against foure general captaines sent by King Antiochus Epiphanes. The first against this Apollonius.
The second against Seron.
The natural frailtie of man feareth to encounter with a strong enemie, but true confidence in Gods help, which is euer assured in a good cause, giueth courage and getteth the victorie.
Not only the Iewes resisted Antiochus innouations in religion, but also diuers other nations reuolted and rebelled, because he commanded al to leaue their former rites.
Praying, fasting & other workes of penance are the best armour in holie warres for religion.
Publike place of prayer was first in Silo, Iosue 18. after in Maspha, 1. Reg. 7. lastly in Ierusalem.
Confidence in God proveth his assistance.
Altars, temples, & statues of false Gods made of stone, and set vp in the temple were now destroyed.
The temple was purged two yeares & somewhat more after the prophanation. Our Sauiour Iesus, observed this feast being instituted long after the Law of Moyses. Ioan. 10.
In this chapter is mention of ten battles in which Iudas or his brethren Ionathas and Simon were victors.
The first against the Idumeans in Acrabathane.
A good and pious captaine cherisheth and comforteth the weake souldiers: and encouraged al to shew their fortitude.
Men that presume of their owne strength without commission from lawful auctoritie haue not Gods assistance.

When our Saviour was carried in his infancie by his mother into Aegypt, the idols of that countrie lost their power. And the inhabitants were specially blessed, and afterwards very many believed in Christ, and sincerely served him.

Both Iewes and Christians understand this prophecie of the conversion of the Aegyptians to Christ. For there were sometimes many Christians in that countrie, yea manie most excellent Saints, S. Paul, S. Antonie, S. Hilarion, & innumerable others.
The holie Prophet, of noble bloud, was not disobedient, nor ashamed to goe naked, because nothing is more honest then to obey Gods commandment. S. Ierem. in hunc locum.

God to shew the vanitie of al hope, that is reposed in men or in worldlie things, layeth the meruelous and miserable mutations of temporal great Kingdoms before the eyes of his people. That we may see, and admire his merciful providence, and our owne follie, when we trust in the helpe of our selves, or of other men, who can not defend themselves from ruine and ignominie. Our only refuge must be to God, in whom is al true hope, helpe, safetie, and happines, temporal and eternal.
The first commination was against the Assyrians, and specially the Babylonians.
Cyrus King of the Persians, a people of smal power, & of the Medes, of great strength.
The seuenth prophetical commination was against the Idumeans.
The ninth against the cheefe rulers of Ierusalem. Sion situated on a hil, and often called a mountaine, is here called a vale, for the afflicted state wherin it was in the captiuitie.
This Sobna had some office about the Temple, but by craftie intrusion and vniust vsurpation rather then by lawful induction, was very couetous & ambitious: so it is Gods iudgement fell into miserie.
Tyrus was an iland, as Ezechiel also describeth it.
The Tyrians reioied in the Iewes captiuitie, & therefore God punished them with like captiuitie of 70. yeares.
Diuersitie of states which is now in the world, shal cease at the general iudgement, and al men shal receiue according to their desertes.
But before he arrived there he had news of his armie in Iurie, & fell into intolerable and desperate diseases.
Al this was but feyned repentance.

Antiochus began to persecute the Iewes in the yeare 143, and dying this yeare 149, it appeareth that his persecution dured about six yeares, or somewhat more: agreeable to the answer of the Angel Dan. 8. v. 14. that it should indure 2300 dayes, which make six yeares, & almost foure months.
Bloud of the grape (wine) and iuyce of mulberries doe incite elephants to fight. As some kinde of bloud or smell thereof doth incite hounds to hunt.
S. Ambrose highly commendeth the fortitude of this souldiar, putting himself in so present danger of death, fighting for religion.
This vsurper with his complices deuised false accusations against Iudas and the rest, to incense the King against them.
Among the Scribes the Assideans were first consulted, being as learned as the Pharisies, or anie other, and indeed more sincerely religious.
This Nicanor was the most terrible enimie against Iudas, but was at last slaine by him.
Of the renowmed actes of the Romanes, other Historiographers haue also written largely: especially Liuius, Diodorus, Iustinus, Florus, Varro, Plutarchus, and manie others.
Polybius li. 5 writeth that Antiochus had 102 elephants in his warre against Ptolomeus, & therefore it is not to be merueled that he had 110 against the Romans.
Though Rome was then gouerned by two consuls: Yet one only ruled euerie day in their course, not both in one day.
This happened about a yeare after the death of Nicanor.

Strongest men are not free from first motions of perturbation, but reflecting vpon their owne infirmitie, & confiding in Gods prouidence, take courage in a good cause, being assured either of temporal victorie, or of eternal glorie.
The mightie may fal in the fight of men, but Iudas his fortitude proued & confirmed by former heroical acts, with prosperous successe, was now perfectly consummate by this most glorious end.
To reuenge or punish faultes in due measure, & other right circumstances, is a special vertue, moderating mans defence of his person, honour, or right, without crueltie, or remisnes.
Euil counsel how soeuer it happeneth to them that follow it, is euer hurtful to them that giue it.

Neer the end of the world, manie forgetting the law of God, & nature, wil rage in extreme furie against others, persecuting & murthering one another, specially the wicked afflicting the good: which our Saviour describeth Mat. 24.

The Prophet and faithful people confessing Gods benefites, and perfect performance of whatsoever he promiseth, or determineth, conforting their desires to his pleasure, say Amen, as well in prosperitie as in adversities.
After the rejection of the Iewes the Gentiles shal be converted to Christ.

In the time of grace given by Christ his whole Church singeth this and other like canticles of praises. Al Christians have one citie, the Catholike Church, signified by Sion, fensed with wal and bulworke, of faith & good workes.
A prophecie of the general resurrection of al men. Some in glorie, some in miserie.
Tyrants are called serpents for their subtle powerful malice, and barres, because they hold men fast inclosed in bondage. And for the same reasons the divel is called a serpent, and a barre.
By Ephraim is understood the kingdom of Israel, whose first King Ieroboam was of that tribe. After that the tenne tribes were carried captives God delivered the two tribes out of imminent danger.

Because Isaias & other holie Prophets often & much vrged, not only the people, but also priests to keepe Gods commandments, and to expect his mercie, they scornfully repeate the same words, deriding such exhortations & desperately giuing themselves to al wickednes, as if they neither feared death nor hel.

As husbandmen dispose their workes in order: so God sometimes worketh miracles, sometimes giveth benefites, sometimes sendeth afflictions: and greater to some then to others. But none are continually afflicted without intermission.

Ariel signifieth the lion of God: So Jerusalem called Ariel is threatened with destruction, which happened first by the Babylonians. Again more miserably by Titus & Vespasian, 40 years after Christs death.
Scribes and Pharisees pretending knowledge of Scriptures, can not read Christ in the Prophets, because these Books are sealed and they have not the key.
The Gentiles could not read Christ in these Books, because they knew not letters of the holy Scriptures.
You that trust in your own counsels and forces, or in other mens, and not in God, shall find the miserable event of your follie.
This claritie in sunne and moone shall be after the general resurrection.
Christ will exercise his severe justice in the general judgement, he shall bid the damned go into everlasting fire. Mat. 25.
It often appeareth that when evil men seem most secure, they fall into sodaine calamities.
Both this Prophet, and afterwards Jeremie admonished the Jews not to trust in the Egyptians, but they contemning this admonition showed in their deeds that they distrusted God.
In the meantime God destroyed the army of Sennacherib besieging Jerusalem. 4. Reg. 19.

Some expound this whole passage of Ezechias, but so great effects were not fully verified in them, but as in figure only of Christ. S. Jerome and other Christian Doctors understand it of Christ, who hath most perfect judgement and justice; and of his Apostles, and other Pastors of his Church who by unction of the Holy Ghost, participate more abundantly of Christs grace. Christian people receive benefits of peace, rest, protection, refreshing in soul, & conscience, & other spiritual comforts in Christ, by the mysteries of the new Testament.
Sennacherib spoiled the kingdom of Israel, and al Iuda saving Jerusalem, which he also besieged, reproching and despising God: but himself was therefore spoyled, and despised.
Fidelitie in performing promises of good things temporal and spiritual.
God willeth as wel gentils that were farre of, to come neere, as also the Iewes that were his peculiar people, al to attend, that he wil destroy this whole world, before the general Iudgement.
No defence of strong places shal saue anie men from destruction in the day of Iudgement.
In the meane time, as a figure thereof, Sion shal be destroyed: and therefore the metaphorical destruction following, rather pertayneth to the state of the damned in the next world.
An euident prophecie of the conuersion of Gentiles. In whom the Church shal continually spring & flourish.
Christ leauing al logical arguments, proued himself to be Messias by his works: If ye beleue not me (sayth our B. Sauiour) belieue my workes. Joan. 10.
That which the Prophet had foretold by way of prophecie, now he recordeth by way of historie, & therfore these foure next chapters are inserted in the fourth Booke of Kings, ch. 18, 19, and 20.
Such is the blindnes, and malice of infidels, that they confound things divine.
Manie in Samaria serued false Gods which could not defend them: the others serued God almightie who for their good suffered them to be afflicted.
Renting or cutting of garments was a ceremonie, to shew internal sorow.
We haue here a manifest example that the merites of Saints do profite the liuing: God protecting Ierusalem not only for his owne, but also for his seruant Dauids sake.
After the Assyrians were slaine, King Ezechias was visited with a dangerous sicknes, to keepe him in the feare of God, left much ioy should bring him into obliuion of his dutie.
In this Canticle the King reciteth his afflictions of mind, for that he should part out of this world, in the flowre of his age.

In this honourable embassage Ezechias reioyed immoderately and vnwisely shewed his treasures, which therfore the Prophet reprehended. And vpon this occasion prophecied that the Babylonians should carie away al his treasure, and his children into captiuitie.
Al flesh is grasse, and al the glorie therof as the floure of the field. But the word of our Lord abideth for euer.
This is manifest of Christs preaching in the mountaine: & of his Apostles, and Apostolical preachers.
Gods powre and beneuolence in creating & gouerning al this world, is a signe that he both can and wil bestow these great benefits of grace vpon al nations.
From the beginning the law of nature, afterwards the law of Moyses taught that he which made the world is God.

Gods expostulation with Ilands, & other Gentils liuing in idolatrie, which implieth a prophecie of their conuersion to Christ, perteyning amongst others very particularly to our great Britanie, the greatest Iland of Europe.

Abraham hath not knowne vs.] The faithful people considering their owne great, frequent, & inueterate sinnes, with the extreme calamities whcrinto they were fallen for the same, supposed that their progenitour Abraham (whom God had particularly called out of his countrie) & Iacob (of whose twelue sonnes the whole nation was propagated) did no longer acknowledge them for their children, because they had so grieuously offended God, & were not worthie of anie fauour. Al which notwithstanding, yet they hoped in Gods inseparable mercie, that his diuine goodnes, being Creatour of al, who had elected the for his peculiar people, brought them out of Egypt, and often deliuered them from sundrie afflictions, would againe reduce them from captiuitie and as their merciful father, remit their sinnes, and relieue their miseries: though Abraham, Iacob, and other Patriarchs had iustly reiected them as lost children. This being the proper literal sense of this place, according to S. Ierome, and other ancient Doctours explication, it maketh nothing at al for the old and new heresie of Vigilantius, & Luther, denying that Saints in an other life doe know what is done in this world. For albeit the Patriarchs in zele of iustice, did not acknowledge their carnal posteritie (because of their great sinnes) for their children, yet they knew their state, as S. Augustin (li. de cura pro mortuis) interpreting this, and other places of holie Scripture, teacheth, partly by relation of such as passed from hence to them; partly by holie Angels; and especially by diuine inspirations. As it is cleere that Abraham knew the state of poore Lazarus, & of the rich glutton, describing what ech of them had deserued, and consequently receiued. Much more both the old Patriarchs, and al other Saints, in eternal glori. know ech other, though neuer seen nor knowne before in this world, as S. Gregorie teacheth li. 4. c. 33. Dial.g. The glorified Saints see also in God that which perteynerh to their clients, that pray vnto them in earth, so farre as God doth ordaine, more cleerly by light of glorie, then Prophets see by light of prophecie: as S. Augustin teacheth. But touching the manner, he saith it exceeded the reach of his vnderstanding, how Martyrs doe helpe those whom it is certaine are holpen by them. So discoursing at large of the vncertaine manner, sheweth that there is no doubt at al of the thing it selfe, that Saints in heauen doe know mortal mens necessities, heare their prayers, and helpe them by theyr intercession and merits; which he confirmeth also li. 20. c. 2. i. cont. Faust. Tract. li. Ioa Ser. 1. de Sanctis. Likewise S. Ierom against Vigilantius, S Gregorie li. 3. Epist. ep. 30. d. 7. ep. 1.26. li. 9. ep. 38. and others in manie places.

If Moyses and Samuel shal stand before me.] As before God reuealed to this Prophet Ieremie, that the people should assuredly be punished, and therfore prohibited him (ch. 7. v. 16. ch. 11. v. 14. & ch. 14. v. 11.) that he should not pray for them: so here he confirmeth the same determinate sentence of their punishment, saying: If Moyses and Samuel shal stand before me, my soule is not towards this people. That is, though not only Ieremie (a zelous holie Prophet now liuing) but also Moyses and Samuel (departed from this world long before) shal pray for this people, yet they shal not escape the designed punishment, for their great sinnes. By necessarie consequence of which confirmation, is also proued, that Moyses and Samuel, after their death, both could and did sometimes pray for the same people. For otherwise the particular mention of these Prophets, were not to the purpose; if they neuer did, nor could pray for them. And wheras the English glosse (in the Genoua Bible) supposeth Gods meaning to be, that if there were anie men liuing moued with so great zele towards the people, as were these two, yet he would not grant the request, for as much as he had determined the contrarie, it is euident that Ieremie, Ezechiel, Daniel, and some other Prophets then liuing, had also great zele, very like to the others, and therfore this text speaketh not of others like vnto them; but as S. Ierom (in his commentaries vpon this place) S. Chrysostom (ho. 1. m 1. Thess. 1.) and S. Gregorie (li. 9. Moral. c. 12.) vnderstand it of Moyses and Samuel themselues, being in assured happie state, where their former great zele and charitie were now greater, and more perfect then in this life.
Christ according to his humanitie is the seruant of God, by whom al other seruants are redeemed, none els being able to satisfie for themselues, much lesse for others.

Such hautie and couetous mindes as expect their Messias to be a worldlie and warlike conquerour, shal be frustrate of their vaine hopes: Whereas Christ our Sauiour both by example & doctrine teacheth the contrarie, willing his folowers to be poore in spirit, meeke & humble in hart.
Christ calleth and indueth sinners with grace, without anie precedent good worke or desire in them, euen as he createth of nothing.
In al tribulations & persecutions Christ protecteth his seruants, not suffering them to be tempted further then they may resist if they wil.
God made Cyrus conquerour of Babylon, not for his owne sake, but for Israel, that he might release their captiuitie. For God perpetually disposeth of Kingdoms for the good of his Church.
The sinne of Adam contaminating al mankind was not purged by anie: Al Patriarches, Priests, Prophets, & al others sinning til Christ the innocent Lambe came to take away the sinne of the world.
Gods election preuenteth mans good endeauour for without grace none could returne to God.

Al that is here sayd of the vanitie of idols & foolish impietie of idolaters, may be referred (faith S. Ierom) vnto heresie, and heretikes, who artificially deuise the fictions of their owne doctrines. The Catholike Church of Christ shal be euer free from such idolatrie: quite contrarie to Protestants assertion that the Church should haue fallen & been in idolatrie manie hundred yeares together.
In al this prophecie of the Church of Christ the Prophet alludeth to the historie of Jerusalem destroyed by the Chaldees, and reedified by permission of Cyrus & Darius.

Because al Kings that reigne among the Iewes were anointed with oyle, Cyrus is called Christ, though he was not ordayned with this ceremony of anointing. S. Ierom noteth that Cyrus finding his name long before prophesied by Isaias, became very beneuolous to the Iewes.
The Prophet contemplating Christ as in a figure, now eleuated more in spirit, prophecieth of Christ only, who built his Church vpon a sure rock.
Not Cyrus (faith S. Ierom) but Christ the Orient starre, prophecied by Balaam, Num. 24. whom the Sages came to adore from the East. Mat. 2.

Bel or Belus, called also Saturnus, was of such estimation, that they offered to him in sacrifice not only men that were taken captiues, but also their owne sonnes. Nabo, otherwise called Dagon, was an especiall idol amongst the Philistims. 1. Reg. 5.
Babylon not hitherto ouercome at last was brought to miserie and destruction.
Gods people contaminating themselues with sinne, were suffered to fal into ignominious captiuitie; but God giueth them grace of repentance, & then seuerely punisheth their vnmerciful afflicters.
By waters in the Hebrew phrase is here vnderstood the fountaine, or spring, the Prophet speaking to those that were of the tribe of Iuda.
These promises of reconciliation to God, and of peace, perteine to the penitent, not to the obstinate in impietie.

Al nations shal be conuerted to Christ: some Iewes in the primitiue Church, and many neere the end of the world. This word from afarre doth conuince (faith S. Ierom) that the Prophet speaketh of al nations to be conuerted to Christ.

The Church of the old Testament seemeth to be forsaken, but God answereth that he can not, & wil not forget, nor forsake his Church, which indeed is al one in the old and new testament, only differing in state.
God did not divorce the Synagogue for her iniquities. Christ will omit no ordinary means but for her sake will indure ignominious afflictions. Al which she contemning shall perish.

Abraham and Sara are proposed for examples to be imitated, being noble progenitors of the Iewes. S. Paul exhorteth his countrie men, and in them al Christians: Remember your Prelates which haue spoken the word of God to you. Heb.13.
Communication with infidels in spiritual things is in no case lawful, as S. Paul shows by this text, 2. Cor.6.
That manie hearing the truth preached doe not believe it, is by their obstinate free wil, because they doe not obey the Gospel.
Our Saviour died and was buried where the wicked were commonly punished: Yet was buried richly & honourably by Ioseph of Arimathia & Nicodemus.

In the old testament Gentils were barren bringing no fruit to God, but in the new testament they are fruitful, & the Iewes are barren, til the later end of the world, when they also shall be fruitful.
As it is assured that the world shall never be drowned again with water, so it is, that the Church of Christ shall never be suppressed.
Grace is offered to al, but those only are iustified, and replenished with more grace and spiritual guifts, that cooperate, doing that which in them lieth, desiring and thirsting iustice.
The beginning of Gods seruice is to forsake the way of wickedness, and returne to our Lord, and he wil haue mercie on him: and to our God, because he is bountiful to forgiue.

Iudgement is a right resolution to doe Gods wil, and iustice is the perfect performance thereof. Under the name of Sabbath is understood the obseruation of al the law. Those that of free election choose good things not commanded, deserue greater reward.

To be barren without children was ignominious amongst the Iewes in the old testament. But the Prophet foresheweth, that Christian Eunuchs liuing virgins, or continent, should not be ignoble but more glorious and haue a better name then sonnes and daughters: an euerlasting name which shal not perish. The ancient holie Fathers vniformely vnderstand this prophecie of such as vow perpetual chastitie in the Church of Christ, preferring that state before Mariage.

Iust men dying seeme to the wicked to perish. But they are gathered to the happie societie of other blessed soules. And commonly God so taketh away the iust, when he wil punish the wicked people, that they may not see the general calamitie of others.
Those that persist obstinate can haue no remission.
Many sinners are so fast asleepe in their wickednes, that they can not heare ordinarie admonitions: to such therfore Gods Preachers must crie, and not cease to crie, as with a loude trumpet.

Fasting alone without amendment of euil manners, & without other good workes, doth not appease Gods wrath. Yet being ioyned with contrition of hart, and sincere pietie, doth then greatly please him. S. Ierom teacheth how it behoueth to fast effectually from sinne.

No defect in Gods power nor wil, that he delivereth not the faithfull from afflictions, but their sinnes are the impediment; for which he punisheth them, that they may repent, & then he wil deliver them.
No pure man being able to redeeme and deliver mankind from captivitie of sinne, God became man to accomplish this worke.
The Church hath the spirit of truth, and therfore can never erre.

God preventing with grace, everie one must cooperate by gratefully accepting this benefite, and so dispose himself to justification. Only those that come into the Church receive the light of true faith, al others are in darkenes.
This prophecie began to be fulfilled in great part when Christ came.
This was accomplished when the Romane Empire, and other Monarchs & nations received the faith of Christ.
Our Saviour was not, neither needed to be visibly annoynted, as Aaron, David, & others were; but invisibly by God, with oyle of gladnes, above al others, with the Holie Ghost, and with power.
It was a griefe and sorow to the Apostles & first Preachers of Christ, that both the Iewes departed from God, & Gentils stil folowed idolatrie, but shortly after, manie were converted.
True Preachers and Pastours cease not from preaching the truth, for anie threats, terrour, or torment. The word of God is not tied. 2.Tim. 2.
The Church is perpetually visible in her watch-men, the Pastours & Governers thereof.

S. Dionyse supposeth this to be the question of the highest order of Angels, admiring Christs beauty to be so excellent, notwithstanding that he is embrewed with bloud, ascending from Edom into heaven, with triumph after a bloudie victorie.
The Angel or guardian of the Church standeth before the face of God. Christ testifying that the particular guardians doe always see the face of his Father. Mat. 18.

The faithful people supposed that their progenitour Abraham & Iacob did no longer acknowledge them for their children. Yet they hoped in Gods mercie. Saints in heaven doe know mortal mens necessities, heare their prayers, and helpe them by theyr intercession and merits. S. Ierom against Vigilantius, S. Gregorie, S. Augustin and others in manie places confirme this.
The good people, with Priests and Prophets of the old Testament most fervently desired Christs comming.
This can not be meant of the Iewes, but necessarily of the Gentils. S. Paul testifieth of both these places. Rom. 10.
Nothing can be more plainly vttered then is here the doctrine of mans freewil. Likewise of reward & punishment of good and euil workes.
The name of the Iewes shall be execrable. And Gods servants shall be called Christians.
After the general Resurrection the heavens and earth shall be altered in qualities, not in substance.
A prophecie that the Temple shall cease. Act. 7. And sacrifices of the old Testament shall become vnlawful.

The house of Rechabites.] We haue here (according to the state of God Church in the old Testament) a cleare example of religious life, by professing and performing good workes of supererogation, not commanded by God, but piouslie instituted by a holie man called Ionadab. Which, not only himselfe, and his proper children, but also their posteritie manie ages after him, voluntarily obserued by a prescript Rule: Not to builde houses, nor dwel in anie (but in tents) not to sow seed, nor to plant, not haue vineyards, nor to drinke wine. A like rule but not the same in al points, was prescribed by God himselfe (Num. 6.) for such as would voluntarily embrace it. And Elias and Elizeus with their disciples obserued an other forme of religious life: as appeareth 4. Reg. 1. 1. &c. Al which were figures of more perfect Religious Orders in the Church of Christ, consisting in three essential vowes, of voluntarie Pouertie, Chastitie, and Obedience: not commanded but commended, and for the better attayning to perfection counselled by our Sauiour, whereof they are called Euangelical Counsels, obserued by the Apostles, leauing al their worldlie substance, and al desire of hauing anie proper possessions: such also as had wiues leauing them, the rest not marying, and al renouncing their owne wils, subiected the same to Christs wil, following him. Whose example others imitating, this holie manner of life hath stil continued in the Church, as is euident by the Ecclesiastical histories euen from the Apostles time.

Signe Thau vpon the foreheads.] Most of the Hebrew Rabbins, as also the Septuagint, likewise Aquila, and Symmachus translate THAV, according to the signification of the Hebrew word, a signe: as if it were commanded only in general to set one signe or other, not anie determinate certaine signe or marke, on the foreheads of the men that mourne and lament vpon the abominations, that are done in Ierusalem. But as wel some other Hebrew Rabbins, with the Chaldee Paraphrasis, as also Theodotion, and in manner al other ancient Latin Interpreters, whom S. Ierom followeth, translate as in the vulgate Edition, signe Thau vpon the foreheads of the men that mourne, &c. That is, marke their foreheads with IHAV, the last Hebrew letter, which in the old Hebrew characters, vsed euen to S. Ieroms time by the Samaritans, hath the similitude and forme of a Crosse. So expressing a particular determinate signe; as when the Angel slew the first-borne of Aegypt, the Israelites houses were distinctly marked with bloud, sprinkled on the posts of their dores: mystically signifying our B. Sauiours most sacred bloud to be shed for Redemption of mankind: so also this letter THAV bearing the forme of a Crosse, formed in the forehead of the mourners, it signified the Crosse, wheron Christ our Lord should dye, and redeeme vs from sinne. Which signe, as it was then prefiguratiue, and prophetical: so now it is representatiue, and commemoratiue of our Redemption made by our B. Sauiour, on the holie Crosse. That we may truly glorie in Christ, and that with our great Apostle S. Paul, in CHRIST CRUCIFIED: With great reason therefore (1. Cor. 2.) hath the whole Church of Christ singularly esteemed, and continually vsed this holie Signe of the Crosse in al Diuine Mysteries, and godlie workes, euen from the Apostles time.

Is the death of a sinner my wil?] In manie places of holie Scripture it is cleere, that Gods wil is most assuredly fulfilled in al things, whatsoeuer he would: and none can resist his wil, &c. Neuertheles here, and in other places it is also expresly affirmed, that God would haue al sinners to repent, and none to dye in their sinnes; which seemeth to repugne with the former doctrine. For solution of which difficultie, S. Damascen (li. 2. c. 29. de Orthodoxa fide) and other Doctours distinguish Gods wil, which is either called Antecedent, and conditional, so God would haue al men to be saued, as appeareth by creating al to that end, by his frequent admonitions, precepts, threats, temporal punishments, and rewards; and especially by our Sauiours death, and redemption of al mankind, whereby he merited most sufficient meanes, and offereth his sufficient grace to euerie one, that they may be saued if they wil. Otherwise Gods wil is called Consequent and absolute; and so for iustice sake his diuine wil is, that impenitent sinners shal be damned, and eternally punished for their sinnes. As a iust Iudge conditionally and antecedently would haue al men to obserue good lawes, and to liue so long as they can by nature; but absolutely and consequently finding some to be murderers, or otherwise pernicious to the commonwealth, he punisheth them with death.

Against Gog, and the Land of Magog.] Gog and Magog, according to the most common opinion, were the King, and people of Scithia, in the North part of the world, a barbarous, sauage, and cruel nation, the offspring of Magog sonne of Iapheth: wherto the Prophet alluding describeth here, as S. Ierom in this place, S. Augustin li. 20. c. 11. de ciuit. and other Fathers expound it, al persecuters of the Church; most especially Antichrist, and his complices. Of whom likewise, by the same mystical names, S. Iohn prophecieth Apoc. 20. v. 7 that Satan shal be loosed out of his prison, and shal goe forth, and seduce the nations, that are vpon the foure corners of the earth: Gog, and Magog, and shal gather them into battel, whose number is as the sand of the sea.
Antichrist signified by Gog shal persecute the Church neere the end of the world.
In euerie part of the vniuersal Church God wil at last destroy Antichrists power, confounding him, and al his adherents.
Simon the fourth general captaine of the Machabees, & high-priest excelled his bretheren in wisdome, by the testimonie of his father.
Simon had now gouerned the people two yeares, beginning with great difficulties; but henceforth enioyed peace.
He reduced manie Iewes from captiuitie.

When peace was established in al Iurie, and freindship confirmed with the Romanes & Lacedemonians, the whole nation of the Iewes in gratitude towards Simon confirmed him in the office of high-priest perpetually.
The high-Priesthood continued in this familie of the Machabees vntil Herod tooke it from them, selling it for money, and then shortly came Christ the faithfull Prophet.
After Simons death, his eldest sonne Iohn, surnamed Hyrcanus, succeeded him in government spiritual and temporal.

He beginning to governe in the yeare 170 had difficulties the first & last yeares, in the rest they had peace. To be inebriated signifieth no more but to be replenished with drinke competently without excesse.
Out of the booke here mentioned some thinke the fourth booke of Machabees was translated.

This Appendix concerning two epistles of the Iewes was added to the historie of the first booke by him that wrote this second. As they had written before in their afflictions, so now they exhorte their bretheren to be thankful to God, and to celebrate the feast of dedication of the new altar.
In this fire were some miracles. First it was not clear fire, but thick water. Secondly this water being cast on the sacrifice was kindled as fire, so that al mervailed.
S. Ambrose writeth at large of this miracle. Neither the tabernacle nor the ark were carried away by Nabuchodonosor.
This seemeth to be the same Iudas Essenus who with others wrote this epistle.

After the author had written this Appendix to the former Book, he resolved also to add an abridgement of the whole history. God assisting the writers of divine histories, doth not always deliver them from labour in seeking to know the same. So S. Luke wrote the Gospel having diligently attained to all things.

The first part. The persecutions of the Church by Antiochus. Three things make a common wealth to be in good state: Good agreement of principal men, exact observation of good laws, and eminent virtue of the supreme governor.
Other three things do trouble the state: Obstinacy in offenders, inveterate malice seeking revenge against just Superiors, and avarice of princes to rob the holy or common treasure.
A fiery chariot and fiery horses carried Elias. From Elizeus the mountains appeared full of horses and of fiery chariots, no less strange then this vision. See S. Ambrose.
It is the common practice of all traitors to calumniate and defame good governors.
The best remedy against such seducers is by authority of Superior power, not by the people, who are commonly more prone to favour faction then justice.
Where true Religion is abolished, the Priests were not now occupied about the offices of the altar, but the temple being contemned & the sacrifices neglected.
Menelaus brother of Simon of the tribe of Beniamin was not by the law capable of the Priesthood, which only pertained to the progenie of Aaron of the tribe of Levi.
True vertue mourned the common people to compassion, the King himself to teares, the Tyrians to honour the bodies of the innocent with costly burial.
Strange things about the ordinarie course of nature doe ever signifie Gods wrath, for mens transgression, and are admonitions to turne with speed, that we may escape the heavie hand of Gods justice.

Al rites of religion with temples and other holie things are ordained to the Service of God, and for mens spiritual good: & therefore when men cease to serve God, holie things are destroyed, or taken away.
Iudas was the tenth lawful highpriest from the Monarchie of the Grecians.
Sanaballat in the time of Alexander the great procured a temple to be built in Garizim like to that in Ierusalem. An other was built in Egypt by Ananias, both schismatical.
He was excellently learned in holie Scriptures and in al divine and humane knowledge.
To feyne or make outward shew of consent to false religion, is never lawful.
In the old testament none could enter into heaven, but the most just went to Limbus when they died.
Old age (faith S. Ambrose) ought to be the haven, not the shipwrake of the former life.
A promise is properly of a good thing, & bindeth the promiser to do that which is in deed good. Whosoever promiseth, or sweareth, or voweth to do evil, is bond not to doe it.
In al good attempts devout prayer is the first preparation. And no where more necessary then in battel.
A iust and religious cause is the very best helpe, that can be in warres.
A chief citie of Persis, called Elymais.

Antiochus was indeed really and seriously grieved, and truly acknowledged that his affliction was for his sinnes, but he was not truly penitent for the offence committed against God, but only for his owne calamitie: & therefore could not obteyne mercie. So also the damned in hel, know & confesse that they are punished for their sinnes, but haue not true repentance.
This recovering and clensing of the temple was after the fourth battel of Iudas.

Beza confesseth that the feast which our Saviour observed was instituted by Iudas Machabeus after the restoring of Gods true religion. It is very strange that Protestants wil denie these Books to be Canonical: which have so excellent a testimonie by the Evangelist of our Saviours owne fact.
It is never an act of fortitude, but of extreme pusillanimity, when one in temporal miserie killeth himselfe. But is a most heroical act to dye willingly for Gods glorie.
This Lysias also had bene vanquished before.
Knowing that the Patriarches were singularly assisted by Angels, these Machabees in their good cause prayed for Angelical helpe, and had it: but ioyntly with their owne endeavour.
An other Timothee was slaine, ch. 10. An other Apollonius was slayn before.

Let his hairs be changed.] In what manner King Nabuchodonosor was changed is hard to explicate. But omitting other opinions, the most probable & common is, that he was not depriued of his reasonable soule, nor the forme & parts of his bodie substantially changed from the nature of a man: but he was distracted loosing therof reason, & in his owne melancholie imagination & phantasie, thought that he was a beast. And therfore easily refused the conuersation of men, & comforted himselfe with beasts, went naked; his haire growing very much, & couering al his bodie, his nayles in vse extreme long, he went on his hands together with his feete, like fourfooted beasts, did eate grasse as an oxe, putting his mouth to the ground.

Vnto a time, and times, and half a time.] Our Sauiour saying (Mat. 24. v. 22.) that the dayes (of Antichrists great persecution) shal be shortened: and Apoc. 17. v. 10. the great persecutour that is to come, must tarie a short time, it is necessarie to say, that the time of the same persecutour here signified to Daniel, as also before ch. 7. v. 25, & repeted Apoc. 12. v. 14. by these termes of a time, & times, and half a time, can not possibly import any long time. And therfore the ancient Fathers vniformly vnderstand by a time one yeare, by times two yeares, and so by half a time half a yeare. Which is somwhat more cleere in other termes, in this ch. v. 11. by a thousand two hundred ninetie dayes, & v. 11. a thousand three hundred thirtie dayes, & Apoc. 11. v. 3. Two witnesses shal prophecie (Against Antichrist) a thousand two hundred sixtie dayes. Apoc. 12. v. 6. The Church shal be fed in the wildernes, the same number of dayes 1260. But most cleerly Apoc. 11. v. 2. & Apoc. 13. v. 5. this great persecution shal indure 42. months, that is, three yeares & a half.

To desolation.] That is, abolishing so much as is possible the holy Sacrifice of Masse, to the end of that persecution shal be 1290. dayes. Why 45. dayes are added to the former number, is meruelous obscure: neither may we presume amongst diuers expositions, to censure which seemeth most probable. But we are content to goe away with Daniel (v. 9, and 13.) without further searching the profound sense of so high mysteries.
Of the donaryes of the idols, that were in Iamnia, from which the law forbiddeth the Iewes: therefore it was made playne to al, that for that cause they were slayne.
Vnles it had been the doctrine & practise of the Church to pray for the dead, Iudas could neuer haue thought of anie such matter.

It is a holie and healthful cogitation to pray for the dead. The Catholike beliefe, doctrine, and practise of praying for the dead, is so euidently confirmed by this place, that our aduersaries haue no better shift then by denying these Books to be Canonical Scripture. Denial of this doctrine is heresie. S. Augustin noteth this for a special heresie. S. Bernard in plaine termes saith they are heretikes which boldly deny that there is purgatorie after death.

Sometimes those only are counted which were permanent, sometimes others are also counted which came vncertainly. The like difficulties of differences occurre often in the books of Kings and Paralipomenon.
The watch word this night was: The victorie of God.
Alcimus was of Aarons flock but for this apostasie was vncapable of high priesthood, & so Matthathias was ordayned, being of the same progenie and most sincere in religion.
This description of the Assideans by their malicious enemie in calumnious termes, sheweth wel their singular zele and sinceritie in promoting Gods feare.
Bacchus called also Liber, feyned by infidels to be author and god of wine. And therefore drunkards dedicate feasts & temples to him.
Prophane men make their advantage of religious mens good conscience, but Iudas rightly instructed defended his just cause also in the Sabbath.

Onias prayed for al the people. And Ieremie prayeth for the people. Seeing the Prophet Ieremie, and the High-Priest Onias, being in Limbo patrum, did pray for the whole people of the Iewes, it is also certaine that they, and other Saints in glorie, doe of their excellent charitie pray for those that are in this mortal life. S. Bernard admonisheth that a good religious man is like to this Onias who prayeth to God for al the people.

Ieremie persuaded the King to yeald himself to the Chaldees, and not to resist. But now he delivereth a sword to Iudas, exhorting him to fight: according to Gods wil in divers cases, for there is a time of warre, and a time of peace. Eccle. 3.
Gods honour and holie things are first and principally to be respected, before worldlie freinds, though they also must be regarded in due order and place.
Gods clemencie inuiteth to repentance, & his iustice punisheth obstinate sinners.
He Prophecied also in banishment in Aegypt. Ieremie had fiue speciall prerogatiues: He was sanctified in his mothers wombe; a Priest; a Prophet; a perpetuall virgin; & Martyr.
He prophecied not only of the Iewes but also of the Gentils.
God is watchful to performe his word.
God promised not peace in his life, but victorie in his death.
God multiplied Israel in Aegypt, & deliuered them from seruitude. Not of anie desert, but of Gods mere grace Israel was preferred before other nations.
God commanding two things, to flye from euil & to doe good: the Iewes contrariwise left God and serued idols.
Gods intention when he punisheth, is to moue sinners to repentance: afflicting them in this life, that they may escape eternall damnation.
No idolatrie nor other sinne whatsoever can be so great, but God wil remitte it, if the sinner be penitent.
The kingdom of Iuda receiving more benefites was more faultie then the kingdom of Israel, but neither of them excusable.
After the captiuitie manie Iewes returned to God, but especially this prophecie is fulfilled in the Gentils since Christ.
An oth is an act of religion, & lawful, so it be made by God Almightie, not by false Gods, and with three conditions: in truth, not falsly; in iudgement, with due consideration; and in iustice.
Gods providence never suffereth the Church to be destroyed.
Except al requisite conditions be observed in an oth, it is unlawful.
By the lion, the Prophet seemeth to vnderstand Nabuchodonosor. By the woolf Nabuzardan. By the leopard Alexander the Great, or Antiochus Epiphanes.
It is against Gods iust iudgement to omit such things vnpunished.
As serpents can not be hindered by inchantments from hurting men, no more can the furious Chaldees be disswaded from killing, and spoiling, and ransaking the Israelites.
Flesh offered in sacrifice is holy, but profiteth not those that are obstinate in sinne.
Our Lord shewed me, and I have known, and foretold, that he should be betrayed & crucified.
He speaketh only of the wicked, for some relikes remaine.
False Prophets seducing the people, can not excuse them from sinne. For when the blind leadeth the blind, both fall into the pit.
Notwithstanding there were very many grievous sinners, yet in respect of the just the Church is a virgin.

No prayers of others doe auaile for obstinate & impenitent sinners. Moyses and Samuel, after their death, both could and did sometimes pray for the people. S. Ierom, S. Chrysostom, and S. Gregorie vnderstand this of Moyses and Samuel being in blessed state, where their former great zele and charitie were now greater, and more perfect then in this life.
The Prophets of the old testament were zealous.
A potter can make a new vessel of the same clay being misformed, so God can also reforme man being hardened in hart.
Alluding to his owne persecution the Prophet here speaketh expressly of Christ, as S. Ierome sheweth, when the Iewes crucified Christ.
This revelation was made to Ieremie long after those in the former chapters.
Gods grace is euer ready that sinners may conuert if they wil.
Comminations to Ierusalem, especially to the King, euil Priests, & false Prophets.
Gods grace is euer ready that sinners may conuert if they wil.
Every one relying upon his private spirit is a manifest note of heresie.
A Mission of Pasteurs and Prophets was alwayes so necessarie in Gods Church, that whosoeuer commeth without right mission, is a false prophet.
False Prophets may doe false miracles, that is, strange things to deceive others: but can not worke true miracles. The note of right mission is a more secure marke to know true & false Prophets.
The Iewes are reproved.
Though God of his nature is most meeke, yet provoked by sinne he powreth out wrath.
Gods comminations are conditional, if the people persist in sinne, they shal be punished, but if they repent the punishment shal be mitigated.
The Prophet hearing a good thing falsly avouched wisheth it might be so, but warneth the people not to belieue it, because it is false.

They decreed.] Beza in his Annotations (in Ioan 10. v. 22.) set forth in English in the yeare 1603. confesseth that the feast which our Sauiour obserued, was instituted by Iudas Machabeus, and his brethren, after the restoring of Gods true religion by casting out Antiochus his garrison. It is also cleare that this feast was in winter, ibidem, agreeable to the text, in the month of Casleu, which is Nouember, whereas the feast of tabernacles was in September, before winter; and the feast of restauration of the temple after the captiuitie of Babylon, was in Adar (1. Esd. 6.) which is Februarie, between which time and middes of March, was not competent space for those things which Christ did after this feast, before his Passion. And therefore it is very strange that Beza, or other Protestants wil denie these Bookes to be Canonical: which haue so excellent a testimonie by the Euangelist of our Sauiours owne fact.

Onias prayed for al the people. And v. 14. Ieremie prayeth for the people.] As against prayer of the faithful for the dead (Ch. 12.) so against prayer of Saints for the militant Church, Protestants haue no better euasion, when they are pressed with these examples, then by denying the auctoritie of the Books. For seeing the Prophet Ieremie, and the High-Priest Onias, being in Limbo patrum (no holie soules ascending into heauen before Christ) did pray for the whole people of the Iewes, it is also certaine that they, and other Saints in glorie, doe of their excellent charitie pray for those that are in this mortal life. Yet neither doe we Catholikes vrge this place, as though we had no other to alleage, for we shew the same doctrine by other holie Scriptures, Gen. 48. Exo. 32. Iere. 15. Luc. 16. and others. Among others ancient Origen (tomo 18. in Ioan.) sayth, it appeareth that Saints departed from this life haue care of the people, as it is written (sayth he) in the Acts of the Machabees, manie yeares after the death of Ieremie: this is Ieremie the Prophet of God, who prayeth much for the people. Likewise S. Bernard Ser. 1. vigil. Natiuit. Domini, admonisheth that a good religious man is like to this Onias: who prayeth to God for al the people.

If not so worthily.] He demandeth not pardon, as though he suspected any errour in his doctrine or in the history, but of his vnpolished stile in writing. As S. Paul sayth that himself was rude in speach, yet not in knowledge, 1. Cor. 11. v. 6. But we, who by Gods great goodnes haue passed now to the end of this English old Testament; iustly fearing that we haue not worthily discharged so great a work: & in no wise presuming that we haue auoided al errours, as wel of doctrine as historie: much more we acknowledge that our title is rude & vnpolished. And therefore we necessarily, and with al humilitie craue pardon of God & of his glorious Saints, Likewise of the Church militant, & particularly of you, right welbeloued English readers, to whom as at the beginning we directed & dedicated these our endeuours: so to you we offer the rest of our labours, euen to the end of our liues: in our B. Sauiour IESVS Christ, to whom be al prayse and glorie. Amen.
After seventy yeares captivitie the temple shall be reedified, but more fully & more perfectly this Prophecie is fulfilled in Christ and his Apostles.
Together with the two tribes manie also of the tribes of Israel found grace in the desert. When Christ came he redeemed more then the former.
Recidination into former sinnes after remission offendeth God more then the former sinnes, as our Saviour teacheth by a Parable.
Behold thou hast made heaven and earth in thy great strength: no word shal be hard to thee.
When they were in the wildernes newly delivered from Egypt, they committed manie hainous crimes: in murmuring, schisme, idolatrie, & other carnal & spiritual sinnes.
God is faithful in his promises.
Recidination into former sinnes after remission offendeth God more.
God was not the cause of the Babylonians crueltie, but permitted & directed the same to punish the Iewes.
Ieremie and Baruch hid themselves in secret places, as Catholike Priests doe now in England, that they may better exercise their function.
The secretarie cut out the leaves and burnt them by the Kings commandement.
God directed the searchers so they could not find the hidden prophets.
Prophecies are not only certaine when a thing is absolutely foretold, but also when they are conditional.
Not sincerely weeping, but hypocritically feyning to lament the destruction of the Temple and Citie.
Auarice taketh crueltie when nothing els can.
The people sought counsel from the Prophet.
The people disobeyed the Prophets counsel.
They reputed the moone as Queene, and the sunne as King of the starres: more peculiarly men did sacrifice to the sunne, and women to the moone.
Baruch is comforted.
Prophecies against the nations.
Babylon which is northward from Palestine.
Nabo a chief citie of Moab and Cariathaim, Hesebon, Oronaim, and the rest shall be destroyed.
This Prophecie perteineth to Syria, whereof Damascus is the chief citie.
Cedar was one of Ismaels sonnes, and Asor the chief citie of the Agarens, to whom this Prophecie perteineth.
The destruction of Babylon is prophesied.
In al this chapter the Prophet amplifieth the vtter destruction of Babylon.
The fall of Jerusalem is recorded.
Idolatrie which is spiritual adultrie.
First Nabuchodonosor tooke away much treasure, afterwards his capitaine Nabuzardan spoyled al.
Ægypt wherin the Iewes trusted to finde ayde, could not, or at least did not help them.
At home is famine.
As the sea exceedeth al other waters, so the affliction of Jerusalem surpasseth al.
This happened in Samaria, & in the siege of Jerusalem by Titus and Vespasian.
Especially understood of Christ. Matt. 26.
God punisheth his servants not to hurt them but for their good.
The speech of such as deny Gods providence.
Preaching of false Prophets hath brought these evils upon us.
Women being by nature pitiful, were cruel to their own children.
False Prophets were called by the name of Prophets, as they seemed in the world to be.
This pertaineth either to King Iosias by the Egyptians, or to Sedecias taken by the Chaldees. Mystically of Christ our Saviour. S. Aug.
This Prophecie is supposed by many to be Ieremies. By others accounted Baruchs. By al holden to be Canonical Scripture.
Remember O Lord what is come upon us: consider and behold our reproach.
Seeing it was Gods wil they should be under the Chaldees then any other foreine nation.
The temple was not as yet destroyed, but the Prophet speaketh of it as he saw it should come to passe.
The law of Moyses ceased after Christ, but Christs law continueth to the end of the world.
Fabulatores, those that did frame moral examples for instruction of manners, were worthily esteemed in al ages: not such as feyned false and ridiculous Gods.
It is vnpossible to finde true wisdom without Gods grace, & by his grace it is easily found.
By this text, most of the Fathers proue Christ to be God against Iewes, Paganes, and Heretikes.
Wisdom wherof he spake in the former chapter is the law and commandment of God.
Mercie is here called iustice, in respect of Gods promise, for although his promise was of his only mercie, yet the performance proceedeth also from his iustice.
Idolaters imagining that idols had diuine power, foolishly esteemed them of great value & price.
Sparta the chief citie of Lacedemonia called also Lacedemon, and Taramne.
Spartians otherwise called Lacedemonians (by Iosephus and other writers) descended from Abraham, v.21. and were in great league with the Iewes.
The Spartianes had written this epistle before Onias wrote to them, though it be here placed after.
There remained with the Spartiates old writes of genealogies, as Iosephus supposeth. c. 12. c. 3. li. 13. c 9.

Morally in Tryphonis noted the practise of the diuel: who intending to ouerthrow a King or a Kingdome, first seeketh to deceiue the pastours, and to destroy them: especially by errour or other sinne. For (as S. Gregorie teacheth, ho. 28.) if the pastours life be corrupted, his doctrine wil be contemned.
Sparta being the chief citie of Lacedemonia, had manie cities subiect & depending as vpon their Metropolis.

When peace was established in al Iurie, and friendshippe confirmed with the Romanes, & Lacedemonians, the whole nation of the Iewes in gratitude towards Simon, who onlie now remained of Mathathias sonnes, confirmed him in the office of high-priest perpetually or for euer, v.41 that is, during his life, & to his progenie, v.49.
He beginning to gouerne in the yeare 170. ch. 13. v. 41. 42. had difficulties the first & last yeares, in the rest they had peace.

To be inebriared signifieth no more but to be replenished with drinke competently, or aboundantly, without excesse. As Psal. 6. v. 10. the earth inebriated, and v. 11. riuers inebriated. That is, aboundantly replenished without excesse. S. August q. 144. in Gen.
It seemeth that either this Iudas was the first sonne of Iohn Hyrcanus (otherwise called Aristobulus) or rather Iudas Essenus, of whom ch. 2. v. 14. and of whom Iosephus writeth. li. 13. c. 19.
In this fire were foure miracles. First it was not changed into ayre but into water. v. 20. Secondly this water being cast on the molles of sacrifice was kindled as fire v. 21.
Thirdly, it burned also being cast upon stones v. 32.
Fourthly, it was extinguished by the light that came from the altar, v. 34.
Nephthar, sig. in Hebrew deliverie, which is the effect of purification, for the temple & other holy things being purified, were delivered from prophane use.
S. Ambrose, li. 3. c. 14. Offic., writeth at large of this miracle.
This seemeth to be the same Iudas Essenus who with others write this epistle. ch. v. 10.

Three things make a commonwealth to be in good state: 1. Good agreement of principal men amongst themselves, & with the commonalty. 2. Exact observation of good laws. 3. And eminent virtue of the supreme governor with exercise of piety and hate of crimes.

Other three things do trouble the state: Obstinacy in offenders, not content to be corrected. 2. Inveterate malice seeking revenge against Superiors. 3. and avarice of princes, to rob the holy or common treasure.
These virgins remained in places near to the temple, brought up in exercises of piety, fasting and praying, til they were despoused. 1 Reg. 1, v. 22. S. Ambrose li. de virginib.
Bands and chaynes are signes of captiuitie, because they are the very instruments.
Hangmen, or executioners, are Gods instruments, & his seruants in punishing the wicked.
By this Metaphor of eating a book, is signified, that the Prophet received reuelations from God, as appeareth. v. 10 &c.

By this place and the like (Mat. 11. v. 21.) it seemeth that the same grace being offered to diuers persons, some doe accept it, and some doe not: but the cause of difference is by more grace added to the former which was sufficient before, and by this superaddition is made effectual. That God giueth this abundance to some is of his mercie, and that he giueth it not to other is no iniurie. Ro. 9.
Adorning of idols with much gold was occasion that the Chaldees spoiled the citie more eagerly.

After that the Prophet had rested in his house 390. dayes, in which time when so euer he slept, he lay only on his left side, & 40. dayes in like manner on his right side, which was in al 430. dayes, which make one yeare, two months, and fiue dayes, the next day he had this other vision before he went forth to preach.

That which is commonly said of manie is called a Prouerb. False Prophets perswaded the people, that seeing the captiuitie foretold by the Prophets was not yet come, therefore it would neuer come. As heretikes shal deny the day of general iudgement. 1. Pet. 3.

Another general rule is here giuen, That as men alter their manners from euil to good or from good to euil, so they shal be iudged & finally rewarded or punished, according to the state wherin they end this life.
